explanation_used:
1. **Opening statement**: This neuron strongly responds to **mentions of formal institutions, organizations, or official bodies** (such as national governments, political parties, judicial courts, law enforcement agencies, central banks, or major NGOs) often within the context of their authoritative actions, statements, or policies.

2. **Domain coverage**: The pattern appears predominantly in formal news or reporting contexts covering politics, government, law, international relations, and national security. It activates across multiple languages (English, Turkish) when they reference such entities. Examples include "Conservative Party," "State Opening of Parliament," "National Investigation Agency," "US Navy," "central bank," "ACLU," and "Court." It also fires on institutional processes like elections, legislation, trials, and official investigations.

3. **Contrast with low-activation examples**: The high-activation texts consistently contain explicit references to specific, named institutions or their formal proceedings. The low-activation examples lack these; they include informal snippets, personal stories, generic headlines, untranslated foreign text fragments, or content where institutions are either absent or not the focal point of the clause (e.g., "George Floyd's arrest" mentions police indirectly but the text fragment centers on the event, not the institution).

4. **Closing interpretation**: The neuron detects text where formal, often governmental or organizational, authority is semantically central. It acts as a filter for passages grounded in institutional discourse rather than personal, commercial, or general narrative.

results.plain_text: Dashing through the notes, in a one horse open sleigh  What is your favourite Christmas carol and how did it come about? Here’s some trivia about some of the popular Yuletide numbers.  The halls are decked, the baking almost done. And through it all, it has been the melodic carols on loop that have set the tone of the season. With Christmas only two days away, here is some trivia of our Yuletide playlist this year. Joy to the World This is easily one of the most popular hymns of the season. Written by English hymn writer Isaac Watts, the carol bears reference to Psalm 98 in the Bible. First published in 1719 in Watts' collection; The Psalms of David by English hymn writer, Isaac Watts, the words of “Joy to the World” were written as a hymn glorifying Christ's victorious return at the end of the age rather than a song commemorating His first coming. By the late 20th century, this carol was the most-published Christmas hymn in North America. Jingle Bells A crowd favourite at Christmas, ‘Jingle Bells’ was written by James Lord Pierpont and was published in late 1857. Originally known as ‘One Horse Open Sleigh’, it has been claimed by some that the song was initially written to be sung by a Sunday School choir but those who thought the lyrics were too racy, refuted that claim. What might come as a surprise though is that despite the song originally not being intended on having any connection to Christmas, it later came to be associated with the festive season. Rudolph the Red Nosed Reindeer Written by Johnny Marks based on the 1939 story ‘Rudolph the Red-Nosed Reindeer’, published by the Montgomery Ward Company, supposedly it was the writer’s brother in law, Robert L. May, who created Rudolph as an assignment for Montgomery Ward. As you would have noticed, Rudolph is the only named reindeer in the song today, originally the song had an added introduction stating the names of the eight reindeer - Dasher, Dancer, Prancer, Vixon, Comet, Cupid, Donner and Blitzen. Wouldn’t the song be much longer then? But of course, Rudolph would’ve remained our favourite! The Little Drummer Boy Originally known as “Carol of the Drum”, this popular Christmas song was written by Katherine Kennicott, an American classical music composer and teacher in 1941. It was recorded by the Trapp Family Singers in 1951 on the choir’s first LP. Further popularised by the Henry Simeone Chorale recording in 1958, there have been innumerable versions and remakes of the song since. Currently, the rendition by Pentatonix, an acapella group, is extremely popular on the internet. Feliz Navidad A macaronic Christmas song written and released in 1970 by Jose Feliciano, a Puerto Rican singer and songwriter, the song comprises a simple chorus in Spanish. In his 1970 recording of the song, Feliciano plays an acoustic guitar and a Puerto Rican cuarto. Around this time of the year, it is said to be one of the most downloaded songs especially in America and Canada. Silent Night Originally written in German, “Silent Night” was composed by Franz Xaver Gruber in 1818 to the lyrics by Joseph Mohr in Austria. It was acknowledged an imperceptible cultural tradition by UNESCO in March 2011. Recorded by different singers in one too many genres, it is extremely popular especially with choirs. Its fame has hit an all-time high so much so it’s been translated into around 140 languages today. Deck the Halls Despite not knowing most of the lyrics of the song, don’t we all love singing the ‘Fa la la la la la la la la’ bit? A little like Auld Lang Syne, this popular number is not just a Christmas special but a New Years’ carol. Connected with a Welsh tradition that dates back to the sixteenth century, its English version came out only in about the 1860s. Also, this upbeat song like other carols made back in the day were meant to serve as music for dances! We Three Kings Also known as “The Quest of the Magi”, this Christmas carol was written in 1857 by John Henry Hopkins Jr. It was when he was serving as the rector of Christ Episcopal Church in Williamsport, Pennsylvania, that he wrote the song for a Christmas pageant in New York. Unlike most people of his time, it was very surprising that Hopkins wrote both the lyrics as well as the music for this song. God Rest Ye Merry Gentlemen Alternately known as ‘Tidings of Comfort and Joy’, this traditional Christmas carol is listed as a Roud Folk Song in the Roxburghe Collection. Originating as early as the 16th century, this traditional English melody is in the minor mode and is among the more complex yuletide tunes to play. Interestingly, this carol is referred to in Charles Dickens’ A Christmas Carol. The Twelve Days of Christmas Written in 1780, this English carol enumerates a chain of increasingly grander gifts given on each of the twelve days of Christmas in the manner of a snowballing song. Believe it or not but initially it was a chant and had no music. The twelve days in the song are the twelve days starting Christmas Day, or in some traditions, on Boxing Day. Improv versions are common and while the Metal and Bollywood ones have been fairly amusing, we’ve even heard school children sing about their favourite food and their rendition started with “an idli and some chutney!”

results.plain_text: The Ole Miss Rebels and coach Kermit Davis celebrate selection as No. 8 seed into the 2019 NCAA Tournament. Nick Suss, The Clarion-Ledger OXFORD — Breein Tyree hasn't slept since Thursday. A junior guard and first-team All-SEC honoree for the Ole Miss basketball team, Tyree said he's been unable to focus on anything except the Rebels' potential NCAA Tournament seeding since the team's early exit from the SEC Tournament. Now Tyree's wait is over as he, along with his teammates, watched from The Pavilion on Sunday night as the selection committee placed Ole Miss as the No. 8 seed in the South Region, set to face off against No. 9 seed Oklahoma on Friday from Columbia, S.C. To Tyree, Ole Miss' first NCAA Tournament bid since 2015 is more than just a relief. It's a chance to fulfill a lifelong dream. "When I was in high school, kids used to watch in their laptops during class," Tyree said. "Teachers were yelling at kids for watching on their phones and going to the bathroom to watch games. This is where you showcase what you can do. I just thank God for the opportunity." Ole Miss carries a 20-12 record into the postseason and went 10-9 against SEC opponents counting its SEC tournament loss to Alabama. Oklahoma, by contrast, enters Friday's game with a 19-13 record and went 7-11 in Big 12 play. The Rebels fell in the SEC tournament to Alabama 62-57. Ole Miss now awaits it's NCAA Tournament bid on Sunday. Mackenzie Salmon, Clarion Ledger To first-year Ole Miss coach Kermit Davis, Sunday's announcement is a validation of why he came to Ole Miss. Davis has led two other schools to NCAA Tournaments in the past, boasting two NCAA Tournament wins on his resume from his time coaching at Middle Tennessee State. But Davis said one of the biggest appeals of coming to Ole Miss was being able to coach at a school where earning an NCAA Tournament bid without putting all the stress on winning the conference tournament wasn't just a possibility, but realistic. Ole Miss athletic director Ross Bjork echoed that notion. "To have a coach of his caliber, his stature, he deserves to be in a place like this," Bjork said. "He's a great coach. He knows the game, he's well-respected. That was his goal, to get to a place where you know you're in the tournament. You might not get to a conference championship, but you know you're in the tournament because of the body of work. So that was rewarding to hear that from him." Davis said he went into Sunday expecting to be a No. 8 or No. 9 seed based on his team's body of work. Ole Miss has five quadrant one wins this season, and seven of its 12 losses came against fellow NCAA Tournament teams. But Davis also understands that a lot of the team's success deserves to be credited to the players like Tyree and senior guard Terence Davis. Those two veteran guards have combined to average 23.3 points, 8.8 rebounds and 6.2 assists per game this season, earning both players All-SEC recognition. "They told me thank you," Kermit Davis said. "And I said 'No, it's a big thank you back.' That makes you feel good that you're a part of an experience that those two guys have been waiting on and worked really, really hard for. It's two guys that really love Ole Miss. That was really fun to see." Ole Miss' impending trip to Columbia won't be a wholly new experience. The Rebels played in that arena on Feb. 19 in a loss against South Carolina where Tyree and Davis put up 19 points and 18 points respectively. To the senior guard, the draw of playing in a familiar arena in a familiar city can't be denied. "One of our staff members, Connor, he was like 'We might end up in South Carolina' and I was just like 'OK. I'm good friends with those rims,'" Terence Davis said. "Being a four-year guy in the SEC, getting the rims you pretty much play a few games in, you feel pretty good about it." Regardless of context or placement or inevitability, earning a bid to the NCAA Tournament in year one of a new program is an impressive accomplishment. To put the achievement in perspective, it took Tennessee coach Rick Barnes three season to bring the Vols to the tournament, it took Auburn coach Bruce Pearl four years to take the Tigers dancing and South Carolina coach Frank Martin needed five years to earn his first March Madness bid. Those three coaches combined to make 29 NCAA Tournaments at Power 5 conference schools prior to their current jobs and none of them posted winning records until Year 3. For Kermit Davis to do what he did in a year where the SEC sent six other teams to the NCAA Tournament justifies the reason Bjork brought him to Oxford. And it puts a pretty nice bow on the 363 days since Davis held his introductory press conference as Ole Miss' head basketball coach. "It's amazing that it's a year that I had my press conference at The Pavilion," the coach said. "Like I said, for a team that finished last and lost 13 out of 14, picked last and then to kind of do what they did as a No. 8 seed and the body of work to happen at Ole Miss. I'm so happy for TD and DC and Bruce as seniors and Breein and Devontae and Dom, those guys that came back. It's a terrific day for Ole Miss basketball." Are you amped up for Ole Miss' NCAA Tournament bid? Want to know more about the Rebels as they prepare for the Big Dance? Contact Nick Suss at 601-408-2674 or nsuss@gannett.com. Follow @nicksuss on Twitter.

results.actual_text: Australian academic moved to notorious Tehran jail Issued on: Sydney (AFP)
results.plain_text: Australian academic moved to notorious Tehran jail  Issued on:  Sydney (AFP)  An Australian academic held in Iran for more than two years has been returned to Tehran's notorious Evin prison, friends said Friday, prompting fresh concern about her wellbeing. Kylie Moore-Gilbert -- who is serving a 10-year sentence on charges of espionage -- had disappeared inside Iran's prison system a week ago, sparking frantic efforts to learn her whereabouts. "I'm relieved that the Australian government has finally managed to locate Kylie six days after she went missing," friend and fellow Middle East expert Dara Conduit told AFP. "But make no mistake: this is not a win for Kylie." Conditions at Evin are believed to be marginally better than Moore-Gilbert's previous jail at Qarchak -- a women's facility that has been blacklisted under UN human rights sanctions and is notorious for the ill-treatment of political prisoners. During a previous stint at Evin, Moore-Gilbert reported being held in restrictive conditions and needing psychiatric medications for "gravely damaged" mental health. Friends believe she is now being held at the same ward as before, a facility controlled by Iran's hardline Islamic Revolutionary Guards Corps. Australia's foreign ministry has said securing her release is an "absolute priority", but was forced to admit this week that her whereabouts were unknown. "We do not accept the charges upon which Dr Moore-Gilbert was convicted, and want to see her returned to Australia as soon as possible," the ministry said after ambassador Lyndall Sachs was able to visit her in Qarchak Prison on October 19. Throughout Moore-Gilbert's internment, friends and family have become increasingly critical of what they say is Australia's ineffective diplomatic approach. According to Conduit: "Not one iota of progress has been made in her case, despite the government's assurances that Kylie's case is under control." She called Moore-Gilbert's transfer back to Evin "an utter indictment of the Australian government's failure on Kylie's case". "After 778 days, she is back at square one in the prison in which she was originally held." Moore-Gilbert was reportedly arrested at Tehran airport by the Islamic Revolutionary Guards Corps in September 2018 after attending a conference in Qoms. It is believed she was reported by a conference delegate or someone interviewed for her research, which focused on the Gulf -- in particular Bahrain, a crucible for competition between Iran and Saudi Arabia. She is just one of several Westerners being held in Iran on national security grounds. Negotiations with Tehran are notoriously difficult, with governments and families forced to decide if quiet discussions are less likely to antagonise captors, often against a fraught geopolitical backdrop. Iran's complex political and judicial system -- which sees hardliners, reformists and myriad state institutions vying for influence -- can make things more complex still.  © 2020 AFP
